sentences.text: Right.
After that, maybe it-- maybe two or three days, maybe longer.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I saw the truck when coming back.
And I didn't know where-- where-- who was in there.
But then I heard that they through my father out on-- on the steps, on the porch sort of.
And before I realized, he was in the bedroom, my father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in bed.
He was all beaten up.
They tortured him.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they threw him to our house.
And my mother was sitting on the chair next to him.
And he was talking to her from the-- lying on the bed.
And again, I went to the window, and I sat down on the floor to listen what my father was going to tell my mother.
And he said that when they took them away, they took them away to the city.
And they took him into a-- sort of like a cellar, maybe.
And the Gestapo were just with them.
And they called up my older brother first, my oldest brother.
And they took him into another room, and my father said-- with leather belts and with clubs, they were hitting him so terrible.
And my father said, that was Gershon, and he was screaming just terrible.
And they wanted my father to hear them.
And when they were finished torturing him, they threw him out.
And then they took my younger brother, Yankel, and they did the same torture to him with these leather belts and God knows what else.
And again, my father heard my second brother screaming just terrible.
And then they threw him out again.
And they took my youngest brother in.
And some of the Gestapos were with them, and the others took them into the torture room.
And he was a 14-year-old.
And they tortured him the same way.
And again, my father said, 'I heard him scream just terrible.' And then they threw him out again to the room.
And then they took my father in to the torture room and they did the same thing to him, beating him and torturing him.
And then my father said, then-- then they threw him out.
And my father was-- I guess they took my brothers away, and my father was too sick to get up.
And all I heard him say that he-- he was-- he was dying, so they sent him back to die at home.
And then he says to my mother, call all the girls here fast.
I want to make a blessing.
And I don't know where my two older sisters were, but my youngest sister, Susan, she brought in Susan.
And I was in the room already.
So I got up.
I heard him say.
And my mother brought in my younger sister.
And my father put his hand on my head and gave me a blessing.
And then he put his hand on my youngest sister, and he blessed her.
And he just closed his eyes and died.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And of course, I never say my brothers again.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's what [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this war was doing to us Jewish people.
After that, again, I-- I-- my mother was a strong woman.
I never heard her scream or yell, or-- she was just saying, praying, and just said, we've got to keep you girls.
We've got to do something now.
Now that we know what's going on, we've gotta do something to help you survive this.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] then she started to look for different homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before that, a cousin of mine, Bronia Resnik, and she was my mother's sister's daughter from the next village.
And her mother, they handed her in that village.
And the father, of course, which was my aunt's husband, they-- they killed him.
And so my mother took her niece over to our house.
And so now my mother tried to get different homes now to hide.
So we were like separate-- separately, she would find a home for my two sisters and a home for my cousin, a home for us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for some reason, I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess my other sisters were in the village, hiding in different homes.
And my mother and I ended up in the ghetto in Korzec.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in this ghetto, of course, there's no life or death, whatever.
It was bad.
No food, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no clothing, no water, no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just-- just lived there and--
That was in '41.
When-- when they got my brothers and my father, it was in '41.
And--
They came in-- in June.
It was during the summer.
But in the-- in the ghetto was maybe fall, in fall of that year.
They probably told us to go-- to go there, because all I remember is my mother and I were in the ghetto.
I knew that-- my mother got a place where to hide, but it was in Czernica.
So I knew they were there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, in the ghetto, you're not-- again, I don't know, for some reason, I always run.
I was always running to the window.
And I was running to the window, and it was a nice, sunny day.
And I saw all these children playing outside in the park.
And I just ran up to my mother, and I says, why can't I play outside like all the other children?
Of course, she picked me up, and she says, [NON ENGLISH SPEECH].
Because we are Jewish.
It's OK, continue.
What did she say?
She said, because we are Jewish people.
We are born Jewish, and we must never lose hope and always believe in God.
And someday, you'll be free again, and you'll play outside.
Terrible, grim, but there were all only women, children, and older people, older men that were dying from torturing.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't go outside.
We couldn't do nothing, just sit there and-- and wait for the inevitable.
Just--
I don't know exactly how long, but one evening, late at night, my mother woke me up.
And she said, you've got to be very quiet now, because we're going to run away from the ghetto, you and I.
And she said, you mustn't cry, you mustn't talk, because the Germans will catch us.
And she took me.
And it was very late at night.
And we ran away from the ghetto, back towards the village where my sisters were hiding.
And it-- it must have been like maybe 1:00, 2:00 in the morning.
And as we were running, running, and we heard some shouts.
I guess more people were running away, and the Germans from the ghetto above, they were shooting at us.
And my mother said, we have to fall down now, and she covered me up.
And she told me not to move and-- and not to cry until she tells me when we can go again.
So we waited till all the shooting stopped, maybe a little longer yet, til it was all quiet.
And my mother told me, OK, we can run again.
And she picked me up.
And we ran again, and we ran away all the way to the village.
Which village?
Korzec.
It's a small town.
My mother, again, found the house, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because she knew-- she knew a lot of these people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she asked if they would hide us.
And they said, well, maybe just for a few days, two or three days, because they were scared, too.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] so here we were at the house, and now she brought my sister, Susan.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my other sisters were still in a different house, hiding.
And of course, when we were hiding there, it was somewhere in the corner, so no neighbors could see us.
Because if neighbors would see us, they'd-- they'd report us to the police immediately.
So these people that were trying to protect us had to be very careful as well for their lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my two sisters, Bayla and Leah, were hiding in a different place, which my mother knew where they were.
And I don't know how long we were in this house.
And then one morning, late in the morning, the woman comes up to my mother, and she says, I have bad news for you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Gestapo-- actually, at first, it was the Ukrainian police, got my two sisters.
Somebody told them where they were hiding.
And they picked them up from this house and they were taking them sort of towards the city.
And on the way to the city, they saw a ditch, this Ukrainian police, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which they were like the Germans in uniforms.
And they saw this ditch, and they told my-- my-- they stopped, and they told my sisters to get out, to get undressed, and to go into the ditch.
They shot them there and killed them.
And this is what this lady told my mother.
And I'm listening [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how they were killed.
So now just my mother, my sister, and I, my youngest sister, and I, left alone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, my mother-- I don't know how she-- you know.
She still tried and still hoping that-- that maybe the two youngest ones will survive.
She just told us, pray, kids, pray.
God will help us.
[SOBS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, after that, these-- these women didn't want us anymore.
They told us-- told my mother, you have to go and look again for other places, because we cannot stay there anymore.
My cousin was hiding in a different place.
And after that, I haven't seen him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at night, my mother would go out and find another place, and then we've have to go there.
And-- and this Ukrainian peoples would keep us-- I don't know-- two, three days, and tell us to go-- to go, we must go.
And my mother, again, at night, would go to every house-- to as many houses as she can, many villages far away, and come back at night and tell us we got to move again, go again, hide again.
And then one evening, she went out again to look.
And like, she'd come back like, you know, during the night, like maybe 3:00, 4:00 in the morning.
And here I woke up, it was early in the morning, maybe-- I don't know, maybe 5 or 6 o'clock.
And I saw-- we were all on the floor, and I sort of looked, and I-- I see my mother's not here.
And I thought, gee, it's-- it's morning already.
She should be here.
She's not back.
And I don't know, I guess I fell asleep again.
And sure enough, later that day, this lady, the woman of the house, she comes up to us.
And now she tells me, she tells us, I have bad news for you.
[SOBS] She said, last night, she says, your mother was looking for a place-- and it was snowing that night-- and I guess she saw the Ukrainian police following her.
And so she ran [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] down the snow on the fields.
And she saw up to-- saw a tree, and she went up to a tree, climbed up to a tree to hide.
And of course, this police followed her footsteps all the way to the tree.
And they said that-- she said they took her down.
And they were torturing right there.
She said that they were taking needles in under her fingernails, pushed right through.
And they said, you must have more children.
You must have more children.
You have to tell us.
And of course, she wouldn't.
And then this lady said, and they took her away, and they gave her over to the gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So now my sister and I, [SOBS] the two of us just left alone.
And I-- I just couldn't believe it that my mother is gone.
Is no more with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were two young children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was just-- I-- I-- I just didn't know what we're going to do now.
And now the lady tells us again.
She says, you know, you kids can't stay here, because they'll find you, and they'll take you away.
And you've got to go.
So now Susan and I, the two of us, had to leave that house.
Now I had to start looking for a place where to hide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's winter now, and we're hungry, and we're cold [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and all alone and mustn't say you're Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And running and running again.
And, you know, my mother was running to look for place.
Now I started to run.
And we'd find a house, which they would keep us for a day or two.
And again, they'd say, you know, you've gotta go.
You can't stay here.
And this was going on and on.
Find-- find a house to sleep over.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then one night, I went to look again.
And I would leave my sister now, and I'd go look for a place, and then I'd find it and come back and say, OK, we've got to move now to go somewhere else.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then one night, I went into this village-- to this house, I'm sorry.
And-- and this woman, she recognized me, or she knew that I, you know, was a Jewish kid.
And two guys walked into the house.
They weren't dressed in uniforms, but they were Ukrainian guys that were supporting the Germans.
And this woman-- they came in, they saw me, and the woman says, there's-- this one is a [UKRAINIAN], which means a Jewish kid.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And so the-- well, these two guys, they said, oh, well, we've got to take her to the police, to the Ukrainian police, which are like the Germans, you know, working together.

And it was late at night, and we walked out, and one guy was in front of me, and I was in the middle, and the second guy was behind me, walking to the police station or police house, to the-- this head policeman house.
And I thought to myself, well, now-- now only my sister is going to be left alone, my youngest sister.
And I was walking, and somehow, I remember I wasn't so scared, because I thought, well, God is with me.
God is with me.
[SOBS] And we walked, and sure enough, they took me right into this policeman's house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like an office in the house.
And I walked in, and sure enough, he-- he was sitting, this head Gestapo or Ukrainian, was sitting at the table, and a lot of other policeman around the table.
And bottles of liquor were on the table.
And I could see that he was drunk, or he was drinking.
I don't think I knew what drunk meant, but I saw he was drinking.
And-- but he didn't have his hat on, you know.
The others looked like scary, and he sort of, like, is drinking a lot and not have the hat on.
And they told him, here's a Jewish kid walking around.
So he says to me, where are your parents?
Where's your mother?
Where's your father?
I says, they're-- they're killed.
They're not here.
You have brothers.
I says, I had.
They're not here anymore.
They're killed.
Sisters?
I says, There were two.
I have no more sisters left.
You've got nobody with you?
I says, no, I'm all alone.
Of course, I wouldn't tell him about my youngest sister.
I says, no, I'm left all alone.
And he's laughing, and he's-- and again, they're drinking, and he's still questioning me.
I can't remember what else he was asking me.
Then he says to the two guys, ah, let her go.
And so I left, and they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I thought, well, God is with me so far, didn't take me away.
So I went back where my sister was hiding, and I came back to her.

And again, I had to go out and look again for the next day, next night, look for some more places to hide and some more [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] going from house to house, from-- and cold and freezing and hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But somehow, like, I don't know where I got the strength or what.
I was doing what my mother was doing, looking for other places.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
And we continued hiding and hiding.
And I just-- I guess I just couldn't take it anymore.
I don't know for how long it was going on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to my sister, you know, I want to go see the mayor.
And-- because I missed my mother so much, so very much.
I just didn't know how to-- to continue this.
And I said to my sister, I says, I want to go see the mayor and tell him to give us over to the Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So I asked her if she wants to go, and she says, I'll go with you.
And so we went now, we're going out to the mayor's.
And I'm going to tell him who we are and let him give us away to the Gestapos, and maybe I'll see my mother yet.
And sure enough, we come to the mayor's house.
And he was there and his wife.
And I told him who we are.
And I says, only two of us are left.
I want you to give us over to the Germans.
And maybe my mother's still alive, I says, because I want to see my mother.
We can't go on like this.
And he sort of looked at his wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they looked at each other.
I don't know what they were saying to each other or if they did say anything.
And-- and the-- he says, are you kids hungry?
I says, yes.
And he told his wife to give us something to eat.
And just then I look out the window again.
And I see the Gestapo's coming towards his house.
They stopped.
And-- and then the mayor just then looked at-- through the window.
And he says, sure enough, the Gestapos are coming to his house.
And it was snowing, you know, it was a cold day.
Cold day.
And he told his wife, take those kids.
Let them out through the back door.
And she grabbed us, and she opened the back door, and she let us out fast, before the Gestapo came into the front door.
So here again, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] here on the streets, looking again where to hide, where to go.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's getting late at night.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we're walking, and we're walking, and I think my sister was freezing.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, I said, well, God again, didn't take me away again.
[SOBS] I'm on the streets one more time.
And somehow, as a child, you know, you just-- I don't know.
You go and you-- you know, I think I had my mother in me, because I still continued going and looking where to hide, where do go.
And then we were so tired.
And I saw on a farm a big haystack.
And I said to my sister, let's go into this haystack, make a hole, and we'll sleep over here, because I can't walk anymore.
And so we went into the haystack and covered ourselves up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then early in the morning, very early in the morning, the farmer, I heard him come with his huge forks, farm forks, to pick hay.
And I can hear him going with those forks way down.
And I motion to my sister.
I said, cover your eyes, because it's going to poke her-- our eyes out.
And so we both covered our eyes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we waited for him to get all the hay out.
And thank God he never touched us with those huge forks.
And then it was all quiet, and we heard he left.
It was daylight now.
We walked out from this hay-- big haystack, walking again, looking again [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for hiding places.
